title: Singin' hinnies
subtitle: Northumberland griddle scones with currants
excerpt: Traditional Northumberland griddle scones studded with currants and cooked on a dry griddle rather than in the oven. The name comes from the sound the fat makes as the dough hits the hot iron. Makes eight.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): singin' hinnies are recorded in the Northumberland chapter as a traditional griddle scone made with flour, lard, currants, and milk. The name is documented as deriving from the singing (sizzling) sound on the hot griddle and the local term of endearment 'hinny'. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: griddle-baked scones are recorded as a Northern English and Scottish domestic preparation, distinct from the oven-baked southern scone.

recipe.batchNotes: Cook in two batches if the griddle is not large enough for all eight at once.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten warm from the griddle. Split and butter while hot.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: dry griddle over medium heat; the surface should make a sizzle when the dough touches it

content.text: Singin' hinnies are cooked on the hob rather than in the oven, which makes them faster and distinctly different from an oven scone. The griddle needs to be dry and hot; no oil, no butter. A pat of cold butter added the moment they come off the griddle, into the split, is the correct finish.
Mix, roll, and griddle — about 10 minutes per side

Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Stir in the {{dried-currants}}. Add the {{whole-milk}} a little at a time, mixing with a knife until the dough just comes together; it should be firm enough to handle without sticking. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and roll or pat to a circle about 1 cm thick, then cut into eight wedges or rounds with a floured 6 cm cutter. Heat a dry griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat until a pinch of flour dropped on it turns pale brown in 20 seconds. Cook the hinnies for 8–10 minutes per side until the surface is pale golden and the centre is set (press lightly; it should spring back). Split while warm and butter generously.
